result:
The first thing that comes to me is the silence. Not an empty silence, but a thick, velvet one—the kind you hear in the minutes after turning off a car engine on a country road at night. It’s in that silence that the noise of being alive becomes most apparent. The hum of the refrigerator, the distant sigh of a train, the blood rushing in your own ears, a sound like a seashell held up to them. We spend our lives building buffers against that silence: playlists, podcasts, the chatter of social media, the internal monologue that never, ever stops. But the silence is always there, underneath. Waiting to remind you that you are, at your core, a solitary creature listening to your own machinery run.

I think about the people I have known. Not the grand loves or the seismic losses, though those are there, too. I think of the man who used to run the corner newsstand, who always slipped an extra piece of bubblegum to a child with a comic book. I wonder what became of him. I think of a girl I sat next to on a bus in a rainstorm in 2004; we shared a packet of mints and didn’t speak a word. For twenty minutes, our lives were parallel lines, close enough to touch. Then her stop came, and she vanished into the wet blur of the city. She is a grandmother now, perhaps. Or no longer alive. A ghost in my memory, real and whole only to herself. We are all, to each other, fragments. We see a sliver of a person—the impatient tone in the checkout line, the sudden laugh on the phone—and we build a whole story around that shard. We are all amateur novelists, writing the inner lives of strangers with arrogant, tragic confidence.

There is a tree outside my window that I have watched for a decade. It has taught me more about patience than any philosophy text. In autumn, it lets go of each leaf not with a shriek, but with a shrug. A gentle release. It doesn’t cling. It knows the script. Bare branches against a gray winter sky are not a tragedy; they are a architecture, a blueprint. Then the slow, impossible green of spring, so tentative you think you might be imagining it. By summer, it is a riot of shade and rustle, full of hidden birds. It has lived through my joys and despairs, indifferent and magnificent. It is simply being a tree. I aspire to that: to simply be a person, with the same unselfconscious grace. To occupy my own space without apology or frantic explanation.

We carry such strange, specific weights. The exact feel of a lost dog’s ear. The smell of a library book from childhood—paper, glue, and the ghost of other people’s houses. The crushing, mundane guilt of an unreturned email from three years ago. The luminous peace of a kitchen lit only by the stove light, waiting for a kettle to boil at 2 a.m. These are the things that compose a life, far more than the certificates on the wall or the resume bullet points. We are museums of fleeting sensations, curated by a forgetful and sentimental curator.

And time—time is the trickster. It stretches in the dentist’s chair and compresses in the arms of a loved one. The last five years feel like a sentence breathed. The five minutes before an important phone call feel like an epoch. We imagine time as a river carrying us forward, but I think it’s more like an ocean. We are bobbing on the surface, and sometimes a current from the deep past—a scent, a few bars of a song—surges up and pulls us under, and we are suddenly ten years old, feeling the exact texture of a carpet under our knees, before we bob back up to the present, gasping.

I think of all the things my hands have held. A newborn’s head, lighter than a thought. The rough bark of a climbing tree. The cold, smooth handle of a shovel at a grave. The sticky hand of a child. The keys to a first home. The weight of a suitcase at the beginning of a journey. Our hands are our primary negotiators with the world. They build, comfort, grasp, and release. They bear the maps of our labor—the nicks, the scars, the changing texture of skin.

What is the point of it all? The question hangs in the air, the ultimate cliché. But clichés become clichés because they are true. Perhaps the point isn’t a destination, but a quality of attention. The act of noticing. The decision, in a world bent on distraction, to truly *see* the way light slants through a dusty window at four in the afternoon. To really *listen* to the story a friend is telling, not just wait for your turn to speak. To taste the bitterness of the coffee, the sweetness of the apple, as if for the first time. To feel the ache in your lower back not just as pain, but as a testament to a body that has carried you all this way.

We are here so briefly. A spark in the long, dark night of the cosmos. But what a spectacular accident! Stardust that learned to look up at the stars and wonder about its own origins. To build cathedrals and write sonnets and plant oak trees we will never sit under. To invent laughter, and bread, and music. To feel the profound, confusing bond of love for another temporary collection of atoms.

Maybe it’s enough to be a witness. To stand in the rain and feel it. To be heartbroken and know that this, too, is a form of aliveness. To be joyful and not apologize for the lightness. To look at the chaos and the beauty and whisper, “I was here. I saw this. It mattered to me.”

The silence is back. But now it feels like a companion. It holds the space for all of this. The thousand words are almost up, a tiny container for an ocean of thought. All that remains is the quiet, the tree outside the window, the next breath, and the humble, magnificent act of paying attention.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
